title: The Invisibility Test
desc: A young Mipedian's first invisibility exam. The stakes are higher than they look.

steps.text:
The testing ground at the Sands of the Unseen is a flat expanse of white sand — chosen because it shows footprints clearly and because the ambient Mugic in the area amplifies invisibility. Or exposes its failures.

A young Mipedian stands at the starting line, visibly nervous. Which is ironic, given the test is about becoming invisible.

"The exam is simple," the proctor explains. "Cross the testing ground without being detected. I will attempt to locate you using sight, sound, and sand-reading. If I can identify your position at any point during the crossing, you fail."

The young Mipedian takes a breath. Her scales shimmer — then she vanishes. Mostly. You can see a faint outline where the light bends slightly wrong, like heat distortion. The proctor narrows her eyes.

"She needs coaching," the proctor murmurs to you. "Watch where she fails and tell me which aspect needs work."

options.label: Watch for visual flaws in the invisibility
Watch for sound and movement tells

steps.text:
You focus on the visual. The young Mipedian is decent — her core body vanishes well, the scales refracting light in a convincing simulation of empty air. But the edges betray her.

Her tail. The tip of her tail lags behind the rest of the invisibility field by a fraction of a second. When she moves, there's a brief flash of scale-color at the tail's end before it catches up. It's subtle — you'd miss it if you weren't looking — but the proctor sees it too.

"Tail control," you report. "The field drops at the tip when she changes direction."

The proctor nods. "Common in young casters. The tail is the furthest extremity — hardest to maintain field coherence at distance from the body's center."

She calls the student back. The young Mipedian reappears, flushed and anxious.

"Your tail," the proctor says. "You're casting from your center outward, and the field thins at the edges. Try casting from the extremities inward — wrap the tail first, then work toward your core."

The student tries again. This time, the tail vanishes first. The rest follows. The crossing is cleaner — not perfect, but improved. The faint shimmer at the edges is gone.

"Better," the proctor says. "Again. Twenty more times and you'll stop thinking about it."

You close your eyes and listen. The Sands of the Unseen are almost perfectly quiet — no wind today, no wildlife, just the faint hiss of settling sand grains. In this silence, every sound is a signal.

The student's footsteps are light but present. A soft compression of sand, barely audible. You track her movement — left, straight, slight pause, right. She's weaving, trying to be unpredictable, but her stride length is consistent. You could map her path blind.

Then: a breath. Just one, slightly too sharp — she's concentrating hard, and the effort is physical. The proctor turns toward the sound. Caught.

"Breathing," you report. "She's holding her breath and releasing in bursts. The bursts are audible."

The proctor calls the student back. "Your breathing. You need continuous flow, not hold-and-release. Hold-and-release creates pressure spikes — audible in quiet environments."

The student practices breathing — slow, continuous, in through the nose and out through the mouth with no variation in flow. It takes twenty minutes before it becomes natural.

On the next crossing, the breathing is silent. The footsteps are still faintly there, but quieter — she's adjusted her weight distribution too. The proctor tracks her for the full crossing and only locates her once, near the end.

"Pass," the proctor says. "Barely. Practice daily. Invisibility is a muscle — it atrophies without use."

The student reappears with a grin that lights up the testing ground.
